summary: Gruber explores the surprisingly confusing world of USB-C power adapters, prompted by the HomePod Mini's 14.3 update that added support for 18W chargers alongside the included 20W charger. He explains that wattage alone is misleading — what matters are the specific voltage/amperage configurations a charger can output and a device can accept. He recounts his own experience being burned by Apple's 29W charger looking identical to the 30W charger but lacking the 9V×3A output the iPad Magic Keyboard needed, resulting in dramatically slower charging. The post argues that Apple's near-identical charger designs and unhelpful labeling make this confusion inevitable for even knowledgeable users.
key_points: The HomePod Mini's 14.3 update added support for 18W chargers, fixing a confusing situation where two nearly identical Apple chargers had different compatibility
Maximum wattage is misleading — what actually matters are the specific voltage×amperage configurations a charger can supply that match what a device accepts
Apple's 29W USB-C charger looks identical to the 30W charger but has severely limited output profiles, causing the iPad Magic Keyboard to charge at only 40% speed
Apple's charger labeling is a 'veritable crime against accessibility' — the 18W charger doesn't even state its wattage, requiring users to calculate from voltage×amperage specs
money_quotes: The only way to tell Apple's new 20W charger apart from their old 18W charger is to look at the hard-to-read small print (light gray text on a white background, a veritable crime against accessibility).
If you ever toss the 20W charger into a bag or drawer along with an Apple 18W charger, you needed an extraordinary amount of knowledge to know which charger the HomePod Mini required.
If you make the perfectly reasonable but totally wrong assumption that the stated maximum wattage is all that matters, it sounds like it's about 97% as powerful (29 ÷ 30).
What really matters when an adapter is negotiating with a device are the various voltage/amp configurations that the charger can supply as output, and the device can accept as input.
Plug the Magic Keyboard into Apple's 29W adapter, however, and the best output the charger can supply that the keyboard will accept is a measly 12.48W.
That 29W charger now lives in a drawer with a salty note attached.
key_insight: USB-C charger wattage ratings are dangerously misleading because what actually determines charging speed is the match between specific voltage/amperage profiles, not maximum wattage — and Apple's identical-looking chargers make this confusion worse.
